coredata.dc:title: Zirconia nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots modified carbon paste electrode as electrochemical sensor for determination of antiepileptic drug eslicarbazepine acetate in bulk and pharmaceutical dosage form
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Azab, Marwa M.
Rizk, Mohamed
Abdel Ghany, Nabil A.
Mohamed, Omaima M.
Shaaban Farag, Amir
coredata.dc:description: A new, sensitive and selective electrochemical method has been developed for determination of the antiepileptic prodrug eslicarbazepine acetate (ESL) that is used for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults with epilepsy. It belongs to the class of sodium channel blockers called dibenzoazepine family. A novel modified zirconia nanoparticles carbon paste nanocomposite, carbon dots sensor prepared from banana peels by solvothermal method has been fabricated. The voltammetric method is based on the direct anodic oxidation of ESL at the electrode surface using differential pulse voltammetry. Under the optimum experimental conditions, ESL exhibited a linear relationship in the range of 3.04 × 10-8 to 9.66 × 10-5 M with a lower detection limit of 7.57 × 10-9 M and quantification limit of 2.29 × 10-8 M. The proposed method was validated according to ICH guidelines and was applied for estimating ESL in raw material showing high accuracy and precision with successful application on tablet dosage form.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon paste electrode
Drug analysis
Carbon quantum dots
Differential pulse voltammetry
Eslicarbazepine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images of the prepared CQDs.
Cyclic voltammogram of ESL in 0.04 M PBS pH 6 using CPE at scan rate 50 mV/s.

Differential Pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of 67.49 µM ESL in 0.04 M BRB different pH values using CPE for (a) second peak and (b) first peak using DPV at potential step 20 mV, potential amplitude 100 mV and scan rate 50 mV/s.

Differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of 67.49 µM ESL at bare CPE and its different modifications in 0.04 M PBS solution pH 6 using DPV at potential step 20 mV, potential amplitude 100 mV and scan rate 50 mV/s.

Differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of 67.49 µM ESL at bare CPE and its different modifications after using quantum carbon dots in 0.04 M PBS solution pH 6 using DPV at potential step 20 mV, potential amplitude 100 mV and scan rate 50 mV/s.

Voltammograms for (0.03–96.6 μmol/L) ESL with corresponding calibration curve measured in 0.04 mol/L phosphate buffer solution pH 6 using DPV at potential step 20 mV, potential amplitude 100 mV and scan rate 80 mV/s.
The possible electrochemical oxidation reaction mechanism of ESL on 1%ZrO2/CQD /CPE.
The ratio between the anodic and cathodic peak currents using different scan rates.
An overview of the reported methods for determination of ESL in pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids and tissues.
Statistical comparison between the results obtained by developed method and the reported HPLC method.
Intraday and inter-day precision data obtained by the developed method.
Analytical Eco-Scale score for the proposed method.
Analytical Eco-Scale score for the reported electrochemical method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The score of < 50 represents inadequate green analysis.
The score of > 50 represents acceptable green analysis.
If the score is > 75, it indicates excellent green analysis.
a Analytical Eco-Scale total score = 100- total penalty points.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between our proposed method with the reported electrochemical method concerning ComplexGAPI.

head.ce:title: Zirconia nanoparticles/carbon quantum dots modified carbon paste electrode as electrochemical sensor for determination of antiepileptic drug eslicarbazepine acetate in bulk and pharmaceutical dosage form

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Helwan University, Helwan, 11795 Cairo, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Helwan University, Helwan, 11795 Cairo, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry and Corrosion Lab, Physical Chemistry Dept, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemistry and Corrosion Lab, Physical Chemistry Dept, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new, sensitive and selective electrochemical method has been developed for determination of the antiepileptic prodrug eslicarbazepine acetate (ESL) that is used for the treatment of partial-onset seizures in adults with epilepsy. It belongs to the class of sodium channel blockers called dibenzoazepine family. A novel modified zirconia nanoparticles carbon paste nanocomposite, carbon dots sensor prepared from banana peels by solvothermal method has been fabricated. The voltammetric method is based on the direct anodic oxidation of ESL at the electrode surface using differential pulse voltammetry. Under the optimum experimental conditions, ESL exhibited a linear relationship in the range of 3.04 × 10-8 to 9.66 × 10-5 M with a lower detection limit of 7.57 × 10-9 M and quantification limit of 2.29 × 10-8 M. The proposed method was validated according to ICH guidelines and was applied for estimating ESL in raw material showing high accuracy and precision with successful application on tablet dosage form.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Eslicarbazepine acetate (ESL), (S)-(−)-10-acetoxy-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenz[b,f]azepine-5-carboxamide is a third-generation antiepileptic drug.

ESL is a prodrug which rapidly and extensively undergoes first pass metabolism in the liver after oral administration and converted to the pharmacologically active form eslicarbazepine (S-licarbazepine), which carries out its anticonvulsant activity [1]. ESL as a member of dibenzoazepine family has the dibenzoazepine nucleus bearing the 5-carboxamide.

ESL was approved by the European medicine agency (EMA) and US food and drug administration (FDA) in 2009 and 2015, respectively [2], it is used for the treatment of partial-onset seizures; either as monotherapy or adjunctive therapy, and for the treatment of focal-onset seizures. It is also used as add-on therapy in refractory partial epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and as a part of the antiepileptic class of medications commonly used in intensive care patients to prevent seizures. Recently ESL was accepted as an effective and a well-tolerated adjunctive treatment for adults with Focal-Onset Epilepsy and Primary Generalized Tonic‑Clonic Seizures [3]. Stabilization of the inactivated state of voltage-gated sodium channels is thought to be the mechanism by which ESL exerts its anticonvulsant effect [4,5].

Plasma levels of the parent drug (ESL) usually remained below the lower limit of quantification obtained. After single dose administration of Eslicarbazepine acetate tablet (400 mg) the maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) of eslicarbazepine was 5.2 μg/mL. Renal excretion appears to be the major route of elimination of the ESL metabolites [6].

Various analytical methods have been developed for the determination of ESL, mostly chromatographic methods like HPLC [7–10], LC MS/MS [11,12], stability indicating HPTLC [13], UPLC-PDA [14], and others like Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) [15]. For our knowledge, there is only one electrochemical method that has been reported for determination of ESL in raw material and dosage form and its active metabolite eslicarbazepine base in urine [16].

Among the present analytical techniques, electrochemical method has received extensive attention due to the simplicity of the equipment, with relatively low cost, and rapid analysis. Ease of modification of carbon paste electrodes (CPE) with a wide variety of nanoparticles makes them widely applied for sensitive and selective electrochemical analysis of pharmaceutical drugs [17,18]. For more sensitive electrodes with cheaper materials, electrochemical sensors based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were developed to imply cost effectiveness [19]. CQDs are new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial having particle size smaller than 10 nm [20], they have been employed as electrode modifier due to their wide availability, low toxicity, small size effect, chemical stability, and easy modification. In addition to their optic properties, they have been proven to accelerate redox reactions, where they have more advantages than other carbon-based nanomaterials such as, presenting larger surface area through their abundant functional groups (hydroxyl & amine) providing multiple sites for more effective surface modification and increasing electrocatalytic activity by enhancing intermolecular electroconductivity [21]. Moreover, due to their high electrical conductivity, CQDs accelerate electron transfer involved in electrochemical reactions [22]. Recently, carbon dots nanocomposites with other modifiers such as graphene, chitosan [23], carbon nanotubes [24,25] and metal oxide nanoparticles [26,27] are preferred in electrochemical sensors compared to their individual use. Zirconium dioxide (ZrO2) nanoparticles exhibit unique properties and have gained considerable attention in various fields including electrochemistry. In recent years, ZrO2 nanoparticles have been used as modifiers in the development of electrochemical sensors owing to their advantageous characteristics including high surface area, offering a large number of active sites for interactions with target analytes, chemical stability; making it suitable for long-term sensor operation, electrical conductivity; enabling efficient charge transfer during electrochemical reactions [28]. The incorporation of ZrO2 nanoparticles in electrochemical sensors can enhance their sensitivity, selectivity, and overall performance.

Thus, the obvious advantages of carbon paste based electrochemical sensors with different modifications led to developing a new sensitive, cheap and greener electrochemical sensor, according to greenness metrics (ComplexGAPI & Analytical Eco-Scale) for the determination of ESL in raw material and tablet dosage form based on anodic oxidation of ESL on C-dots@ZrO2/CPE with the participation of two electrons and two protons using phosphate buffer solution (PBS) pH 6.

All reagents used were of analytical grade and double distilled water was used as solvent. Eslicarbazepine acetate standard drug with 100 % purity (batch no. 220748), was kindly supplied from EVA PHARM Co., Cairo, Egypt. Eslizepine® tablets containing 400 mg of Eslicarbazepine acetate per tablet were purchased from the local market.

Graphite powder, zirconium dioxide nanoparticles (ZrO2 NPs, <50 nm particle size, 99.5 % trace metals basis), titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs), Zeolite, copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO2 NPs) and cobalt oxide nanoparticles (Co2O3 NPs), paraffin oil and diethyl ether (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany).

Boric acid (El-Nasr pharmaceutical company, Cairo, Egypt), Glacial acetic acid and phosphoric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) with 0.04 M concentration were mixed to prepare 0.04 M Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solutions of pH 2–9 according to US Pharmacopeia.

Phosphate buffer solutions of pH 6 (0.04, 0.1 and 0.2 M) were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of monobasic potassium phosphate in double distilled water. The desired pH of all buffer solutions was adjusted using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany). Methanol HPLC grade (Sigma-Aldrich, Darmstadt, Germany). Fresh banana fruits were purchased from the market, polyethylene glycol (National Research Center, Giza, Egypt).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and pretreatment of the carbon paste electrode and carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be prepared from different natural sources such as banana peels by hydrothermal method [29,30] In this work, CQDs were prepared by a one-step solvothermal method. Fresh banana peels were washed, cut into small pieces and dried. They were then ground to powder. Then 0.25 g of the banana peel powder was dispersed in 50 mL polyethylene glycol. The solution was vigorously stirred, and the homogenous solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 ◦C for 22 h, then cooled to room temperature. The yellowish-brown solution obtained was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles and filtered using 0.45 µm and 0.22 µm filter membranes. Finally, the synthesized CQDs solution was stored at 4 ℃ for further use and characterization.

The bare CPE was prepared by hand-mixing 0.5 g of pure graphite powder with nearly 0.2 mL of paraffin oil in a porcelain dish. The homogeneous paste was packed carefully into the end of an insulin syringe and smoothed against filter paper till a shiny surface was obtained. A copper wire was inserted into the other end to provide electrical contact. 1–2 % ZrO2, 1–2 % TiO2, 1–2 %Zeolite, 2 % CuO2, and 2 % Co2O3 modified electrodes were prepared by mixing graphite powder with the desired amounts of the nanoparticles as percentage (w/w) in diethyl ether. The homogenous mixtures were then left at room temperature for evaporating diethyl ether, paraffin oil was added and hand mixed till obtaining wet paste. To prepare 1 % ZrO2/CQD/CPE, graphite powder was mixed well with 50 µl of the as prepared CQD and the appropriate weight of ZrO2 NPs on hot plate for 30 sec then was cooled to room temperature and paraffin oil was added to this mixture till wet uniform paste was obtained. 2 %TiO2/CQD/CPE was prepared in the same manner as 1 %ZrO2/CQD/CPE.

A potentiostat 884 professional VA Metrohm connected to viva version 2.0 software was used for all electrochemical measurements. The electrochemical cell was assembled with a conventional three-electrode system where a 1 % ZrO2/CQD/CPE, an Ag/AgCl/KCl (3.0 M) electrode and a platinum wire were employed as a working electrode, a reference electrode and an auxiliary electrode respectively. A bench-top pH-meter (HANNA Instruments, USA) with combined glass electrode was used to carry out the pH measurements. Bench-top sonicator, POWERSONIC410;USA. The surface morphology and the particle size of the prepared CQDs was characterized by Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images using a JEOL JEM-1230 electron microscope, it was operated at 80 kV, Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded using (PerkinElmer Spectrum two, part no. L1600235, serial no: 49754), Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were characterized using a double beam JASCO (V-530) UV – Visible Spectrophotometer using 2 matched 1 cm quartz cuvettes. Fluorescence spectra were obtained on JASCO FP-6200 – Spectrofluorometer using a 150 W xenon lamp and 1 cm quartz cell, the excitation and emission wavelength band passes were both set at 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard solutions preparation and construction of calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para: First Stock standard solution of ESL (3.37 × 10-3 M) was prepared by dissolving 100 mg in small amount of methanol in 100 mL volumetric flask and the volume was completed to the mark using double distilled water, then diluted to obtain the second stock standard solution (3.37 × 10-4 M) and the working standard solution of ESL (3.37 × 10-5 M). Aliquots of ESL stock and working standard solution were added to an electrolytic cell containing 10 ml of PBS buffer of pH 6 to cover the concentration range of 3.04 × 10-8 to 9.66 × 10-5 M. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the peak currents of the detected peak against the corresponding concentrations of the drug followed by computing the regression equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the tablet dosage form
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets of Eslizepine® tab were weighed accurately and ground to a fine powder. A portion of the powder equivalent to 50 mg ESL was transferred into a 50 mL volumetric flask and dissolved in 5 mL methanol then the flask was sonicated for 10 min, and the volume was completed with double distilled water to prepare the first stock solution ( stock solution A ) of 3.37 × 10-3 M. Then, stock solution A was filtered using a disposable syringe filter (0.45 μm), diluted using double distilled water to obtain a second stock solution ( stock solution B ) of ESL (3.37 × 10-4 M). Then diluted with distilled water to get the working standard solution (3.37 × 10-5 M). Aliquots of the ESL were taken into the cell containing 10 mL of 0.04 M PBS pH 6.0 and the voltammograms were recorded with the same experimental conditions that were used for the calibration curve. The nominal contents of the tablets were calculated from the calibration curve or from the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of prepared carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The Morphology of CQDs was characterized by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM), the HRTEM images showed that the prepared CQDs were well distributed with spherical shapes and separated from each other without aggregation with a particle size below 10 nm (in the Range of 2–8 nm). The size distribution of CQDs was performed, whereby most of the particles lie in the range of 2–4 nm as shown in Fig. 1 .

The optical characteristics of CQDs were examined via UV–vis & fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1S) showed a small Peak at 247 nm related to π-π* transition originates from C-C bonds present, and a broad peak observed at 318 nm attributed to n- π* Transitions From C = O or C-OH present on the CQDs surface.

The fluorescence Spectra exhibits excitation dependent emission, and the highest emission was observed at 407 nm after excitation at 310 nm as most C-dots show maximum emission wavelength dependent on excitation wavelength (shifting fluorescence) which is applicable on the prepared C-dots (Fig. 2S). In few cases, the maximum fluorescence wavelength of C-dots is independent on excitation wavelength (non-shifting fluorescence) [31]. CQDs emit blue luminescence when irradiated under UV light. (Fig. 3S).

The FTIR spectroscopy was used to study the functional groups of the CQDs, (Fig. 4S). Obviously, a broad stretching band at 3295.27 cm−1 was observed, which related to N–H and OH group, sharp peak at 2938.78 and 2875.46 cm−1 related to acidic OH and C–H stretching, A peak at 1455.7, 1411.33 and 1332.72 cm−1 may be related to C–H, acidic O–H and alcoholic O–H bending, 1256.63,1204.53 and 1083.36 cm−1 belongs to the C-N and C-O stretching, and the peak at around 1033.32 belongs to C–H bending vibrational.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of the studied analyte revealed two oxidation peaks at about + 0.21 V and + 1.46 V with one reduction peak at + 0.12 using phosphate buffer (PBS, pH6) indicating the reversibility of the second peak, where the potential scan ranging from 0.0 to + 1.6 V at a potential step of 20 mV, potential amplitude of 100 mV, and scan rate of 50 mV/s. Blank shows no peaks in both directions as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Cyclic voltammograms showed two anodic peaks as mentioned above. Different experimental factors influencing the oxidation behavior of ESL were studied and their effect on both peaks were considered carefully to select the most appropriate peak for ESL quantitation. Such factors include effect of pH and buffer types, Effect of carbon paste electrode modification, and effect of scan rate using 67.49 µM ESL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and buffer types
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of pH on ESL oxidation was studied using 67.49 µM ESL and Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) was used. CPE was immersed in Britton-Robinson buffer (BRB) solution at pH values ranging from 2 to 9. Two anodic peaks appeared in the pH range from 2 to 7 which disappeared in higher pH values, referring to the protonation in low pH. By increasing the pH value, the peak potentials were shifted to lower values indicating that ESL oxidation process is pH dependent which can be explained according to Nernest equation (1) [32],
(1)
E
f , e f f
0
=
E
f 0
- 2.303 m R T / n F p H
The midpoint potential varies by an amount of 2.303 mRT/nF per pH unit. In the commonly seen case where m = n, this corresponds to ca. 59 mV per pH unit at 25 °C. A higher current response was obtained at pH 6 for the second peak. The selected pH for subsequent work was pH 6 for both peaks at 1.424 V and 0.179 V for the first and second peak respectively as found in Fig. 3 .

Different buffer solutions were examined at pH 6 such as BRB and PBS. The results revealed that PBS showed well-defined peaks with higher current response for both peaks than BRB when used as supporting electrolyte. Then different molarities of PBS at pH 6 were examined to detect its effect on ESL oxidation, and 0.04 M phosphate buffer showed the highest peak current, thus: it was selected as the optimum pH.

According to Nernst equation (2),
(2)
Ep = K - ( 0.059 y / n ) p H
where y is the number of H+ ions, and n is the number of electrons. By plotting pH versus peak potential, a linear relationship was obtained with regression equation of E(V) = -0.0584 pH + 0.5275, (r2) = 0.9995 with slope 0.0584, and E(V) = -0.0439 pH + 1.6375, (r2) = 0.9981 with slope 0.0439, for the second and first peaks, respectively. It was obvious that the slopes were extremely close to the slope value of Nernst equation (0.059 per pH unit). These results revealed that the number of electrons is equal to the number of protons for both peaks as illustrated in Fig. 5S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon paste electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: The ease of fabrication and modification of CPE with plenty of nanoparticles, low background current, wide potential window, chemical inertness, low cost, and suitability for sensing undoubtedly represent CPE advantages to study the electrochemical behavior of ESL. The electrochemical responses of different types of modifications were studied using DPV. According to Fig. 4 , the oxidation peak current of ESL on the surface of CPE modified with 1 % zirconia nanoparticles (ZrO2 NPs) was higher than that obtained using the electrodes modified with 2 % ZrO2 NPs, 1–2 % titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2-NPs), 1–2 %Zeolite, 2 % copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO2-NPS), and cobalt oxide nanoparticles (Co2O3-NPS).

Since, CQDs nanocomposite with other modifiers enhance the electrochemical response and improve the catalytic properties of the original modifier, the effect of CQDs nanocomposites with 1 %ZrO2 NPs and 2 % TiO2 NPs on the peak current was studied. ZrO2 nanoparticles used for the modification of CPE enhance electrochemical response through increasing the electroactive surface area of the electrode and also ZrO2 has exceptional electronic characteristics, thanks to its low band gap and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. In addition, due to its high hydrophilicity and thermal stability, ZrO2 significantly enhances the electrochemical performance of the electrode and electrode wettability [28]. An enhancement in the current response with these nanocomposites compared to the single modification was observed due to the abundance of many functional groups on the surface of carbon dots in addition to the tunability of ZrO2 NPs which facilitate the ease of composite preparation, allowing the design of tailored sensor and improves the catalytic properties of the original modifiers. CQDs nanocomposite with 1 %ZrO2 NPs showed the highest peak current which can be ascribed to the disruption of higher concentrations of ZrO2 nanoparticles on the graphite-ZrO2 interaction and electron flow [33]. Hence, it was selected as optimum working electrode modification as mentioned in Fig. 5 .

As shown in Fig. 6S, the electrochemical response of 67.49 µM ESL at 1 %ZrO2/CQD/CPE in 0.04 M phosphate buffer of pH 6 in the range of (10–100) mV/s using CV was studied with increasing scan rate values to study the kinetics of electrode reaction revealing that the best current response was obtained at scan rate 80 mV/s.

By plotting the logarithm of the second oxidation peak current (log Ipa) against the logarithm of the scan rate (log v), a linear relationship with the following regression equation: log Ipa = 0.7735 log v –0.7825 (R2 = 0.999) was obtained. This shows that the oxidation reaction is diffusion-controlled electrode reaction with a contribution of the adsorption as shown in Fig. 7S.

The ratio between the anodic and cathodic peak currents provides information about the reversibility of the process, values from 0.806 to 0.853 were found. From the obtained results, the system appears to be reversible as the values are close to 1 (Table 1 ).

The linear relationship between the peak current (Ipa) and the square root of the scan rate (v ½) according to Randles-Sevcik equation equation (3),
(3)
I p
=
( 2.69 x
10
5
)
n
3 / 2
A
D
1 / 2
C
ν
1 / 2
Where Ip is the anodic or cathodic peak current, n is the number of electrons passed per molecule of analyte oxidized or reduced, A is the electroactive surface area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient of analyte (cm2/s), v is the scan rate (V/s), C is the concentration of analyte in bulk solution (mol/cm3). also reaffirm the reversibility of this system as mentioned in Fig. 8S.

It was observed that with increasing the scan rate, the first oxidation peak current (Ipa1) increased, and the peak potential (Epa1) shifted towards the positive direction as present in Fig. 9S, which indicates the irreversibility of this electrode reaction.

A good linearity was observed from the plot of peak potential versus the logarithm of scan rate (log ν), with the corresponding regression equation of Epa1 = 0.051 log ν + 1.3287 (R2 = 0.9989), for this first peak. On plotting peaks potential versus the log ν for the second peaks, we get regression equations of Epa2 = 0.0368 log ν + 0.139, (R2 = 0.9974) and Epc1 = -0.0102 log ν + 0.1331, (R2 = 0.9977). The number of electrons transferred in the oxidation of ESL can be calculated by Laviron’s equation (4) from the slope.
(4)
Ep =
E °
□ + 2.303 R T / α n F
[ l o g □ R T
K °
/ α n F + l o g □ ν ]
Where R is gas constant 8.314 J.K.mol−1, T is the temperature (298°K), α is the electron transfer coefficient, n is the number of electrons, F is Faraday constant (96485c mol− 1). By assuming α = 0.9 for oxidation peak 1, we can get n = 1.28 approximately 1, and for oxidation peak 2, the value of α can be obtained according to the slopes of Ep vs. log ν [34] using the following equation (5):
(5)
ka / k c = α / ( 1 - α )
where ka and kc are the slopes of the plot of Epa vs. log ν and Epc vs. log ν, equal to 0.0368 and 0.0102 respectively. The α was calculated to be 0.78, so n was found to be 2.06 nearly 2. The results obtained from pH and scan rate demonstrate that the oxidation reaction of ESL at 1 %ZrO2/CQD /CPE involves one electron transfer process and one proton in the first step, and two electrons with two protons in the second step, the suggested mechanism of oxidation of ESL illustrated by Scheme 1 was proposed based on the reported mechanism of carbamazepine oxidation, which belongs to the same class of benzodiazepine having a similar tricyclic structure [35].

Differential pulse voltammograms were recorded from 0 to 1.6 V with the following parameters: pulse amplitude, 80 mV; pulse width, 10 ms, covering the concentration range of 3.04 × 10-8 to 9.66 × 10-5 M (0.009–28.626 µg/mL). The anodic peak current of the second oxidation peak was plotted versus the concentration of the drug (µM) to construct the calibration curve, and the corresponding regression equations was computed.

The method was validated according to the International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) guidelines. The following validation parameters were determined: linearity and range, accuracy, precision, selectivity, limit of detection and limit of quantification.

The linear relationship between the oxidation peak current and ESL concentration was shown in the following DP voltammogram as mentioned in Fig. 6 . It shows linearity within the range from 3.04 × 10-8 to 9.66 × 10-5 M (0.009–––28.626 µg/mL) with a derived linear equation y = 0.436x + 0.1634 (R2 = 0.9997).
The accuracy was determined using the mean % recovery of the standard drug and it was found to be 100.38 %.

Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of ESL were calculated using the following equations. LOD = 3.3 sb/m, LOQ = 10 sb/m; where, sb is the standard deviation of the peak currents (for five runs) of 0.03 µM ESL and m is the slope of the calibration curve. The achieved LOD and LOQ were 7.57 × 10-9 and 2.29 × 10-8 M (0.008 & 0.023 µM), respectively.

All analysis of selected samples were always made in five replicates (n = 5) where the results calculated and presented as the confidence intervals
x
¯
± st 1-α (where
x
¯
is the arithmetic mean, s the standard deviation, and t 1-α the critical value 2.015 of Student's t-distribution for five repetitions at a significance level α = 0.05 (with 95 % probability).

To accomplish the validation study of our ESL sensor, different interferences were studied for minor substances commonly found in tablet dosage such as tablet formulation excipients (lactose monohydrate, magnesium stearate, croscarmellose sodium, corn starch) and it was found that all these compounds did not provide any electrochemical signals within the working potential range, the determination of the drug in the presence of dosage form excipients with high percent recovery and without any interference assures a very good selectivity of the presented method. Also, the effect of interference was studied in the presence of different antiepileptic drugs like gabapentin, lacosamide, phenytoin, brivaracetam and sodium valproate with no interference (Fig. 10S & 11S). From the analytical point of view, these results confirm that our modified ESL sensor allows a selective detection of ESL.
A comparison with other analytical methods developed for the determination of ESL is summarized in Table2 .

RP-HPLC/UV – Reversed Phase High Performance Liquid Chromatography with Ultraviolet detector, LC/UV – liquid chromatography with Ultraviolet detector, LC-MS/MS – Liquid Chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry, HPTLC – High Performance Thin Layer Chromatography, UPLC-PDA – Ultra-Performance Liquid Chromatography with Photodiode array detector, CPE – carbon paste electrode, ZEO – Zeolite nanoparticle, PEG – Polyethylene Glycol.

To determine the accuracy of the proposed method, the results of assay in its pure and pharmaceutical dosage form were compared with a reference method [36]. The reference method is an isocratic HPLC-UV method using a mobile phase composed of (0.005 M ammonium acetate: methanol, 30:70) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min and reversed-phase column C18 with a detection wavelength at 230 nm. The results revealed high accuracy of the proposed method in terms of % recovery. No significant difference was shown by the student’s t-test and variance ratio F-test as shown in Table 3 .
The precision and accuracy data of within- and between-days at four different concentrations are shown in Table 4 .

The concept of green chemistry is gaining more prominence in the field of chemical research. Green assessment methods are tools used to evaluate and measure the environmental impact and sustainability of various processes, products, or technologies. These methods provide a systematic approach to assess and quantify the ecological footprint, resource efficiency, and overall environmental performance. In this study, we employed two recently developed matrices to evaluate the environmental sustainability and greenness of the proposed method and the comparable electrochemical one mentioned in the literature for analyzing the target drug.

One such green assessment method is the analytical Eco-Scale, which focuses on evaluating the environmental characteristics of chemical processes. It considers factors such as solvent usage, waste generation, energy consumption, and occupational hazards. By assigning scores and penalty points to different criteria, the analytical Eco-Scale provides a quantitative assessment of the environmental impact of a process or method. The outcome of an analytical Eco-Scale assessment is a numerical score calculated by subtracting the total penalty points from the overall score of 100 [37]. Tables 5 and 6
present the Eco-Scale score obtained from the proposed and the previously reported electrochemical method, respectively. A higher score indicates a more environmentally friendly approach. Comparing the Eco-Scale scores for our developed method and the reported electrochemical method, our method is classified as an excellent green analysis with a score of 78. In contrast, the reported method is considered acceptable green analyses with score of only 65, primarily due to higher consumption of hazardous reagents.

Another notable green assessment method is ComplexGAPI (Complex Green Analytical Performance Index), which is a comprehensive framework for evaluating the environmental sustainability of analytical methods. It considers various factors such as energy consumption, waste generation, raw material usage, and emissions. By considering the entire life cycle of an analytical method, ComplexGAPI provides a holistic assessment of its environmental performance, allowing for informed decision-making and optimization of analytical processes represented by five pentagrams representing sample collection and preparation, reagents and solvents, instrumentation, method type, and an additional hexagonal field corresponding to pre-analysis processes. The use of red, yellow, and green colors indicates varying levels of environmental impact, with red indicating potentially hazardous steps, yellow representing medium impact, and green reflecting eco-friendly steps [38,39]. By comparing our developed method with the reported electrochemical method, our method demonstrates higher eco-friendliness, as evidenced by fifteen green regions. In contrast, the reported method [16], achieved eight green regions. The hexagonal part at the bottom of the pentagrams, representing pre-analysis processes, the two red regions in this part in our method related to the temperature and energy of the synthesis process used for carbon dots preparation. Regarding the red regions in the reported methods, the red region located in the instrumentation pentagram is due to the high amount of waste produced during sample preparation. The red region in the hexagonal part is related to the high power of the instruments used (Table 7 ).

Both the analytical Eco-Scale and ComplexGAPI contribute to the advancement of green chemistry and sustainable practices by providing objective and quantitative measures of the environmental impact. These assessment methods help researchers, industries, and policymakers identify areas for improvement, promote cleaner and more sustainable technologies, and make informed choices to minimize environmental harm.

In the proposed method, a new zirconia nanoparticles nanocomposite, carbon quantum dots, modified CPE (ZrO2/CQDs/CPE) was elaborated for ESL quantitation. The obtained sensor offers a simple and sensitive approach for determination of ESL in pharmaceutical preparations. The working procedure depends on its direct voltammetric oxidation at the 1 % ZrO2/CQDs/CPE using 0.04 molL-1 phosphate buffer (pH6) as the supporting electrolyte of choice. The proposed method exhibits nearly the same sensitivity when compared to the previously reported electrochemical method. Moreover, this method achieves this high sensitivity while minimizing the environmental hazard, contributing to its eco-friendly nature. This sensor fabrication offers high simplicity through using banana peels as natural source for electrode modification with CQDs, which open the route for more clean and green electrochemical methods for the determination of analytes in pharmaceutical and biological samples, thus, the proposed method sparks potential for future work introducing modifiers derived from nanocomposites of natural sources to be used in electrochemical methods.. Furthermore, this novel voltammetric procedure can be considered as a more green, cheap and simple alternative reference method to check the results being obtained by routine chromatographic techniques.

Marwa M. Azab: Writing - review & editing, Investigation, Supervision. Mohamed Rizk: Supervision. Nabil A. Abdel Ghany: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Omaima M. Mohamed: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Amir Shaaban Farag: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.measurement.2024.115400.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
